explicit_text: I was born in Tokyo, although I moved around so much that I don't really know exactly where. Tokyo is where my life began before I eventually came to the United States in 1917. Even though I left at a young age, Tokyo remains the place of my origin.

explicit_text: Mrs. Fukuda was born in Kumamoto-ken, which is where her roots lie. She mentioned this when we talked about our backgrounds and families in Japan. Kumamoto Prefecture is important because it connects to her early life before coming to the United States.
implicit_text: Mrs. Fukuda was born in a southwestern prefecture on Kyushu Island, which is where her roots lie. She mentioned this when we talked about our backgrounds and families in Japan. That prefecture is important because it connects to her early life before coming to the United States.

explicit_text: I came to the United States in 1917, following my father who had already settled here. Life was tough at first, especially with the Alien Land Law in effect since 1913, which prevented Japanese people from owning or leasing land. I worked various jobs, including as a houseboy in Walnut Creek and San Francisco, and attended English school at Pine Methodist Church. The United States became my home for over 52 years, though I faced many challenges as an immigrant.

explicit_text: When I arrived in the United States in 1917, the Alien Land Law had been in effect since 1913. This law prevented Japanese immigrants like my father and me from buying or even leasing land, which made it very difficult to establish a stable livelihood. My father worked as a share cropper in Oakland because of this restriction, raising tomatoes and cucumbers on land he did not own.
implicit_text: When I arrived in this country in 1917, a law restricting land ownership had been in effect since 1913. This law prevented Japanese immigrants like my father and me from buying or even leasing land, which made it very difficult to establish a stable livelihood. My father worked as a share cropper in Oakland because of this restriction, raising tomatoes and cucumbers on land he did not own.

explicit_text: My father worked as a share cropper in Oakland, raising tomatoes and cucumbers on a 50-50 basis with landlords. Oakland was one of the places where we lived and worked, though he had to move around depending on job availability. It was a tough life, and some years were better than others, but Oakland was a key location in our early years in America.
implicit_text: My father worked as a share cropper in a city on the east side of San Francisco Bay, raising tomatoes and cucumbers on a 50-50 basis with landlords. That city was one of the places where we lived and worked, though he had to move around depending on job availability. It was a tough life, and some years were better than others, but that city was a key location in our early years in America.

explicit_text: My father initially tried to start a business selling canned goods and pickles from Japan in the Hayward area. Unfortunately, the business did not work out well, so he had to quit and later became a share cropper. Hayward was the first place where my father attempted to establish himself in the United States.

explicit_text: When I first came to the United States, I worked as a houseboy in Walnut Creek and San Francisco. My duties included making breakfast, cleaning the kitchen, and doing various chores after school. Walnut Creek was one of the places where I experienced the hard work and discipline of immigrant life in those early years.

explicit_text: In 1922, I came to the United States with my parents, but we were classified as Hi-Imin, or non-immigrants. This classification affected our legal status and opportunities. Despite this, I helped with my parents' business and continued to work hard to build a life here.

explicit_text: At one point, we lived in Wyoming before moving back to Sacramento. It was during this time that I got married in 1926. Wyoming was part of our journey as we searched for stability and community in the United States.

explicit_text: Sacramento became our home after we moved back from Wyoming, where my brother worked for Aki Shoten, a well-known store. I settled here with my family and went back to school to improve my English. I was also a member of the Presbyterian Church in Sacramento, which connected me to the community and to my future wife.
